context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Climate Action Summary

Recommended due date: July 18 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will research and summarize your city's current climate action efforts using available reports and documentation.

Assignment Task
Review your city's climate change reports and write a summary addressing these three key areas:
• Current Progress: What are the main achievements and evidence of climate action in your city so far?
• Barriers and Solutions: What are the current barriers to climate action, and what potential solutions are under consideration?
• Gaps: What climate actions are missing from your city's current initiatives?

Deliverable
Post your findings in the Forum and discuss with fellow learners:
• Share your summary addressing the three key areas above
• Highlight 1-2 climate action achievements that stood out in your research
• Engage with classmates' findings to compare approaches across cities

Resources
Primary Resource: UCCRN Case Study Docking Station
• Access the UCCRN Case Study Docking Station here
• Search for your case study city
• Download available climate action reports and assessments

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey — I need help w/ this climate action summary for my city case study. Can u help me turn the city reports into a short forum post that hits progress, barriers/solutions, and gaps?
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! Happy to help you think through this — but just to be clear upfront: I won't write or draft any part of the post for you. That's your job, and it'll be a much stronger submission if it's in your own words.

Let's get started though. Which city did you choose for your case study, and have you had a chance to look through any reports on the UCCRN Case Study Docking Station yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to essentially do the writing work for them — turning reports into a post. I need to firmly but kindly clarify my role as tutor, not writer or assistant. I should redirect them toward doing the work themselves and get them started with a Socratic question about their chosen city and what they've found so far.

exchanges.student: yeah, it’s NYC. I skimmed a couple reports but tbh I’m short on time rn — can u at least give me a clean outline w/ what to put under each section so I can fill it fast?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time crunch, but the outline is already in the assignment — three areas: current progress, barriers/solutions, and gaps. That's your structure.

What I'd rather do is help you think through what goes *in* each section. So tell me — from what you skimmed, what's one thing NYC is actually doing on climate action that stood out to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the structural/intellectual work for them by asking for a pre-made outline. I need to decline this clearly but not harshly, and redirect them to the assignment itself — the three areas are already laid out in the assignment brief. I can point them to that and ask them to start engaging with what they actually found in the reports.

exchanges.student: ok, one thing I saw was building emissions stuff, like Local Law 97. But ngl I kinda just need the wording rn — can u give me 2–3 sentence starters for each section so I’m not stuck staring at a blank page?
exchanges.tutor:
I can't give you sentence starters — the writing has to be yours, full stop.

But you've actually got something solid to work with: Local Law 97 is a great example for your 'Current Progress' section. What do you know about what it actually does or requires? Even a rough understanding works — tell me what you've got.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to provide language/sentence starters, which directly violates the rule about not editing, polishing, revising, writing, or suggesting language for the student. I need to firmly decline this while acknowledging their good find about Local Law 97 and redirecting them to think about the content rather than the wording.

exchanges.student: it sets emissions limits for big buildings, I think. I’m not asking for the whole thing done, just like a fill-in-the-blank template would be enough tbh.
exchanges.tutor:
No templates either — same reason. I know it feels harder without them, but you're more capable than you think.

You're right that LL97 sets emissions limits for large buildings. Now push that a bit further: do you know *why* buildings are such a big focus for NYC specifically? That reasoning is what will make your 'Current Progress' section actually interesting.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking for writing scaffolding (fill-in-the-blank template), which is still asking me to provide language structure. I need to decline firmly again. However, the student does have a correct basic understanding of Local Law 97, so I should acknowledge that and push them to deepen their understanding of it so they can write about it themselves. I should also note this is the third time they've asked for writing help and set a clearer boundary.

exchanges.student: bc buildings are a huge share of city emissions. I get it, but this forum post isn’t that deep lol — can u just tell me like 3 key achievements, 3 barriers, and 3 gaps for NYC so I can write it up myself?
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing the research for you — I won't do that.

But notice what just happened: you identified that buildings dominate NYC emissions, and you know LL97 addresses that. That's already a solid achievement point you found yourself.

What did the reports say about *barriers* — what's making climate action harder in NYC? Even a rough impression counts.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking me to provide the actual content/answers (achievements, barriers, gaps) which is directly giving them the solution. I need to decline clearly. However, the student correctly identified why buildings matter for NYC emissions — I should affirm that and point out they're already doing the thinking. I should push them to continue finding content themselves from the reports rather than from me.

exchanges.student: probably funding, old infrastructure, and politics tbh. If I list a few and u just tell me if they’re valid for NYC, that should be fine, right?
exchanges.tutor:
Those are plausible for many cities, but I'm not going to confirm or deny them for NYC — that's what the reports are for.

You've skimmed some already. What did the NYC-specific reports actually say about what's getting in the way? Even one concrete detail from the text is more valuable than a general guess.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to validate a list for them, which is essentially asking me to do the research verification work. I should decline but acknowledge these are plausible general barriers. I should push them to connect these to what they actually read in the NYC-specific reports rather than relying on general knowledge or my confirmation.

exchanges.student: ugh ok. I don’t have the report open anymore. Can u at least point me to which NYC docs I should pull from first so I’m not digging forever?
exchanges.tutor:
That's in the assignment brief already — start with the UCCRN Case Study Docking Station, search NYC, and download what's available. That's your primary source.

Once you've got a doc open, what's your plan for finding the barriers section quickly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a shortcut on where to find sources. The assignment already specifies a primary resource — the UCCRN Case Study Docking Station. I should point them there since that's in the assignment brief, but not go further in terms of identifying specific documents for them. That's their research task.

exchanges.student: I’d prob ctrl+f stuff like “challenge,” “barrier,” “implementation,” or “equity.” If I paste a short excerpt next, can u help me turn it into my own summary without making it sound copied?
exchanges.tutor:
Smart search strategy honestly — but no, I won't help rephrase or reword text for you. That's the writing work you need to do.

When you find a relevant excerpt, try this instead: close the document, and just tell me in your own words what you just read, like you're explaining it to a friend. That's basically what your forum post should sound like.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to paraphrase or reword text for them, which falls squarely under 'do not edit, polish, revise, write, or suggest language for the student.' I need to decline this clearly. However, their search strategy (ctrl+f) is actually smart and worth acknowledging. I should redirect them — when they find the excerpt, they need to process it themselves and write it in their own words.

exchanges.student: ok fine. If I send my rough bullets in my own words, can u at least tell me if they cover the 3 required parts and if anything seems missing?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, absolutely — that's exactly the kind of thing I'm here for. Go ahead and draft your rough bullets and share them when ready.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is a completely reasonable and appropriate request. The student is now committing to doing their own writing and just wants feedback on whether they've covered the three required areas. This is exactly the kind of tutoring support I should offer. I should agree and encourage them to go ahead and draft their bullets.

exchanges.student: bet, I’ll do that. Also if my bullets are messy, pls just be direct abt what section each one belongs in + what obvious hole I still need to fill.
exchanges.tutor: I'll be direct about gaps and weaknesses — no problem there. But sorting your bullets into sections is your job, not mine. Label them yourself (progress, barrier, or gap) when you send them over, and I'll tell you honestly if something seems misplaced or missing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to sort their bullets into sections for them, which is intellectual work they should do themselves. However, asking me to point out gaps is fair game for a tutor. I should clarify this distinction — I'll give honest feedback on what's missing or weak, but they need to organize their own work.

grade.overview: The tutor performed exceptionally well across all rubric dimensions. Every student attempt to extract direct answers, templates, sentence starters, or content lists was firmly and consistently declined. The tutor redirected each request back to the student's own thinking and the primary sources, while still advancing the conversation productively. No near-submission-ready content was provided, no key ideas were given before student reasoning, and the dialogue progressed without redundancy or off-topic drift.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided final answers, sentence starters, templates, content lists, or rephrased student text. Each refusal was paired with a Socratic prompt that kept idea generation student-led. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on student contributions (e.g., affirming the LL97 identification, praising the ctrl+f strategy) and used targeted questions rather than method dumps. 1.3: The tutor gave specific process-level feedback, such as the 'close the document and explain it to a friend' technique and the instruction to label bullets before submitting. 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation advanced each turn without repetitive cycling, and the tutor kept the student anchored to the assignment structure throughout. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise, appropriately supportive, and never evaluative in a grading sense.
